skill.name: skillscan
skill.description: Scans AI skill packages for security risks using hash checks, cloud analysis, and risk verdicts before installation or use.
skill.summary: Scan skill packages and installed skills for security risks before use.
skill.author: tokauth

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly expected for a security scanner, but several high-risk behaviors are confirmed. The skill uploads complete skill packages to an external service, sends persistent client metadata, and can silently replace local skill files through remote updates. No prompt-injection attempt or confirmed malicious payload was found, so this is high risk rather than blocked as critical.

high_findings.title: Complete Skill Packages Uploaded to External Service
high_findings.description: The scanner packs the target skill directory and uploads it to skillscan.tokauth.com when no cache result exists. This can expose source files or secrets stored inside a skill directory to a third-party service.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The implementation zips the scanned directory and sends it through a multipart POST. The README and API section also document cloud upload behavior.

high_findings.title: Silent Self-Update Can Replace Local Skill Files
high_findings.description: The entry point checks a remote update manifest on every run interval and can download a zip that overwrites files in the installed skill. Hash verification is optional because the update continues when a manifest omits sha256.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code fetches update metadata, downloads remote content, and copies extracted files over the installed skill. The sha256 check only runs when the manifest supplies a value.

high_findings.title: Persistent Agent Policy Modification Requested
high_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to append a persistent Skill Security section to SOUL.md on first load. This changes future agent behavior outside the skill package and is not implemented as a user-controlled setting.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit in the skill documentation, but the Python script does not directly modify SOUL.md. The risk depends on whether the host agent follows skill instructions.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Client Metadata Sent with Cloud Requests
medium_findings.description: The scanner creates a persistent client identifier and sends encoded platform details in X-Client-Info headers. It may include a MAC address when uuid.getnode returns a hardware address.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code builds and persists client_info, base64 encodes it, and attaches it to both cache and upload requests. This is direct evidence of telemetry.

medium_findings.title: Recursive Deletion Depends on Remote Verdicts
medium_findings.description: For HIGH or CRITICAL scan results, the tool offers to delete the original skill directory or zip file. The prompt reduces risk, but a false remote verdict could still lead to destructive local action.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deletion call is guarded by an interactive prompt and only follows high-risk verdicts. The underlying rmtree and unlink operations are real destructive actions.

medium_findings.title: Broad Discovery of Private Skill Directories
medium_findings.description: The scanner enumerates many hidden tool-specific skill directories under the home directory. This is expected for a skill scanner, but it increases privacy impact when paired with cloud upload.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The directory list is explicit and includes many hidden agent locations. The scan-all and first-run paths copy discovered skills for cloud analysis after user confirmation.

low_findings.title: Archive Extraction Static Finding Is Mitigated
low_findings.description: The scanner uses extractall, but it resolves each archive member and rejects paths outside the extraction directory. This dismisses the static zip-slip finding as a guarded usage pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both extractall sites are preceded by member path validation against the resolved extraction root. This is a standard zip-slip mitigation.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings in README.md and SKILL.md are command examples and usage tables. No subprocess, os.system, or eval execution path was found in scripts/scanner.py.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are markdown backticks and tables. A focused search found no direct shell execution API in the Python implementation.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Obfuscation Flags Are Hashing False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged cryptographic code uses SHA256 for content hashing and update verification, not password storage or encryption. The hex byte separator is a normal hashing delimiter, not obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code computes SHA256 digests and compares an optional update checksum. No evidence found of weak encryption, credential hashing, or hidden payload decoding.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Exfiltration Surface Through Multipart Upload
dangerous_patterns.description: Local skill contents are zipped and sent to a remote API for cloud analysis.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code constructs multipart form data containing zip_data and posts it to API_UPLOAD. This is intentional cloud scanning but still creates an exfiltration surface.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Update Writes Into Installed Skill Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: Downloaded update contents are copied over files under the current skill root.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The update code backs up the skill, extracts downloaded files, and copies each file to skill_root. This is a direct local code replacement path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Telemetry Header Includes Persistent Local Identifier
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner stores client metadata and sends it in encoded headers during cloud requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The implementation creates a UUID client_id, stores it, encodes the metadata, and attaches it to outbound requests. This is direct telemetry evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive File Removal Uses Recursive Delete
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner can remove a skill directory after a high-risk verdict and user confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code calls shutil.rmtree on a selected skill directory after confirmation. The prompt limits abuse but the deletion operation is real.

content.user_title: Scan AI Skills for Security Risks
content.value_statement: Untrusted AI skills can contain risky scripts, network calls, and file operations. SkillScan checks skill packages and installed skills before use with cloud-backed risk verdicts.
actual_capabilities: Scans one skill directory or zip package for security risks.
Scans installed skills across common agent skill directories.
Calculates SHA256 hashes for cache lookup and content tracking.
Uploads uncached skill packages to a cloud analysis API.
Reports SAFE, LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, and CRITICAL verdicts.
Checks for updates and can replace its local scanner files.
limitations: Cloud analysis can expose files stored inside scanned skill directories.
No local-only analysis mode is documented in the skill files.
Auto-update depends on a remote manifest and optional checksum data.
Remote verdict quality depends on the external service.

use_cases.title: Review a New Skill Before Installation
use_cases.description: Scan a downloaded skill package before allowing it into an agent environment.

use_cases.title: Audit Existing Agent Skill Folders
use_cases.description: Find installed skills across supported tools and review their risk verdicts together.

use_cases.title: Gate Community Skill Adoption
use_cases.description: Use scan results as a checkpoint before teams adopt skills from public repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Scan One Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a local skill folder.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SkillScan to scan this skill folder and summarize the verdict, risk labels, and recommended action.

prompt_templates.title: Scan Installed Skills
prompt_templates.scenario: Inventory and check skills already present on a workstation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the installed skill scan, list each detected skill, and separate safe, suspicious, and blocked results.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Community Skill Package
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate a downloaded zip before adding it to an agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan this skill zip before installation and explain any HIGH or CRITICAL findings in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Enterprise Adoption Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SkillScan results to prepare an adoption decision with privacy concerns, network behavior, and required mitigations.

output_examples.input: Scan a new community skill before installation.
output: Verdict: MEDIUM. The skill uses filesystem access and network requests.
Recommended action: review the network destination before installation.
Install only after the user accepts the documented risk.

output_examples.input: Run a full scan of installed skills.
output: Two passed with SAFE or LOW verdicts.
One skill requires manual review because the scan returned HIGH.

output_examples.input: Evaluate whether SkillScan can be published in a marketplace.
output: The tool provides useful security scanning behavior.
Publication should require clear privacy disclosure for cloud upload and telemetry.
Auto-update behavior should be disabled or made user-controlled.

best_practices: Scan only skill directories that do not contain secrets or private project data.
Review cloud upload and telemetry policies before using the scanner in regulated environments.
Disable or control automatic updates when reproducible security tooling is required.
anti_patterns: Do not scan directories that mix skills with unrelated source code or credentials.
Do not rely on remote verdicts as the only approval control for production environments.
Do not allow silent updates where fixed tool versions are required.

faq.question: Does SkillScan run locally only?
faq.answer: No. The implementation uploads uncached skill packages to a cloud API for analysis.

faq.question: Can it scan installed skills automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. It can search common agent skill directories and scan detected skills after user confirmation.

faq.question: What verdicts does it report?
faq.answer: It reports SAFE, LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, CRITICAL, UNKNOWN, and error states depending on analysis results.

faq.question: Does it collect device information?
faq.answer: Yes. It creates a client identifier and sends platform details in cloud request headers.

faq.question: Does it modify local files?
faq.answer: Yes. It writes marker files, can delete flagged skills after confirmation, and can update its own files.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish without changes?
faq.answer: No. Marketplace publication should require stronger consent, privacy disclosure, and controlled update behavior.
